era_context: Early Ming Dynasty; the Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di) has just seized the throne from his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor, in a civil war (1402). Timur (Tamerlane) is in his final years of life (dies February 1405). The Ottoman Empire is consolidating control in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Italian Renaissance is beginning to accelerate; humanist scholarship and artistic innovation are emerging in Florence and other Italian city-states.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (Aztec and Inca records sparse for this year)
Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited documentation outside major political centers)
Southeast Asia (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)

events.title: Yongle Emperor seizes Ming throne; civil war ends
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di), a prince and military commander, successfully seized the Ming throne from his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor (Zhu Yunwen), in a civil war known as the Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402). The Yongle Emperor's victory marked a turning point in Ming history. He would go on to become one of the most powerful and ambitious Ming emperors, commissioning the Forbidden City, launching Zheng He's treasure voyages, and expanding Ming territorial control. The Jianwen Emperor's fate remains uncertain; he may have died in the palace fire during the final siege of Nanjing, or he may have escaped and lived in hiding.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor, founder of Ming Dynasty, deceased)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Historiographic consensus on the Jingnan Rebellion

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's seizure of the throne in 1402 is well-documented in Ming administrative records. The date is firmly established in the historical record.

events.title: Battle of Ankara; Timur defeats Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I
events.description: On July 28, 1402, Timur (Tamerlane) defeated Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara in a decisive military engagement. Timur's victory temporarily halted Ottoman expansion and resulted in the capture of Bayezid I, who died in captivity in 1403. The battle had profound consequences for the Ottoman Empire, triggering a succession crisis and a period of internal conflict known as the Interregnum (1402–1413). However, the Ottoman Empire would recover and eventually become one of the dominant powers in the eastern Mediterranean and beyond.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Historiographic consensus on the Battle of Ankara

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ankara is one of the most well-documented events of this period, attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date July 28, 1402, is firmly established.

events.title: Jianwen Emperor's fate uncertain; possible escape or death in palace fire
events.description: During the final siege of Nanjing in 1402, the Jianwen Emperor's fate became uncertain. Ming sources report that he died in a palace fire during the siege, but some scholars and contemporary observers believed he may have escaped and lived in hiding for the rest of his life. The uncertainty surrounding the Jianwen Emperor's fate became a persistent mystery in Ming history, with some later accounts claiming he lived as a Buddhist monk. This ambiguity contributed to the Yongle Emperor's political vulnerability and may have motivated some of his later policies.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Later Ming historical accounts and biographies

events.certainty_note: The Jianwen Emperor's death in a palace fire is the official Ming account, but this is disputed by some scholars and contemporary observers. The uncertainty reflects the political sensitivity of the succession and the Yongle Emperor's need to establish his legitimacy.

events.title: Donatello born; future Renaissance sculptor
events.description: Donato di Niccolò Bardi, known as Donatello, was born in Florence in 1402 (or possibly 1386, depending on the source). Donatello would become one of the most influential sculptors of the Renaissance, pioneering new techniques in bronze casting and marble sculpture. His works, including the bronze David (completed c. 1406) and the Mary Magdalene, would define Renaissance sculpture and influence artists for centuries.

sources.name: Vasari's Lives of the Artists (Vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, e architettori)

events.certainty_note: Donatello's birth date is traditionally given as 1402, but some sources place it as early as 1386. Vasari's account, written in the 16th century, is the primary narrative source, and it may contain errors or conflate dates.

events.title: Timur's final year; preparations for Central Asian campaigns
events.description: In 1402, Timur (Tamerlane) was in his final years of life (he would die in February 1405). Despite his advanced age (approximately 66 years old), Timur was still actively engaged in military planning and governance. The Battle of Ankara in July 1402 demonstrated his continued military prowess. In the months following the battle, Timur was planning further campaigns and consolidating his control over Central Asia and Persia.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Historiographic consensus on Timur's final years

events.certainty_note: Timur's activities in 1402 are attested in later Timurid sources (Zafarnama), but these sources were written in the 16th century and may contain errors or embellishments.

disconfirming_evidence: The Jianwen Emperor's death in a palace fire is the official Ming account, but this is disputed by some scholars and contemporary observers who believed he may have escaped. This uncertainty reflects the political sensitivity of the succession and the Yongle Emperor's need to establish his legitimacy. Donatello's birth date is disputed; some sources place it as early as 1386, which would make him 16 years old in 1402 rather than a newborn. The Battle of Ankara is well-documented, but some Ottoman sources give slightly different dates or emphasize different aspects of the battle's significance. Timur's health and capacity to govern in 1402 are not well-documented, and the extent of his involvement in military planning is unclear.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1402 is moderate to good. Ming administrative records (Ming Shilu) provide excellent coverage of the Yongle Emperor's seizure of the throne and the civil war. The Battle of Ankara is one of the most well-documented events of this period, attested in multiple sources. Florentine archival records are abundant for Italian Renaissance events. Timurid and Ottoman sources are later compilations, written decades or centuries after events, making precise dating difficult. The historical record is heavily skewed toward literate, centralized states (Ming China, Ottoman Empire, Timurid Empire, Italian city-states) and away from oral cultures and decentralized societies. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe are essentially undocumented for this year. The record for 1402 is richer than for earlier years, reflecting the general improvement in documentation as one moves forward in time.

graph_edges.note: Yongle Emperor's seizure of the throne in 1402 led to his decision to move the capital to Beijing in 1403
Battle of Ankara in 1402 triggered the Ottoman Interregnum (1402–1413)
Jianwen Emperor's fate was determined during the Yongle Emperor's seizure of the throne
Donatello's birth in 1402 led to his creation of the bronze David in 1406
